# Questions in Livestream 82

Live Stream #82: The Censors Are Coming For You!!!

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=670" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:10</a> | Which genealogy was Titus Kennedy using in his Exodus argument? | He said early-date advocates lean on 1 Chronicles 6:33-37, but he regarded that genealogy as honorary and spliced rather than a strict natural line. | He identifies the appealed-to genealogy as 1 Chronicles 6:33-37, where early-date proponents count eighteen generations between Heman and Korah, add one for Solomon, and use a generation length to reach the 1446 BC Exodus date. He argues this does not work because the genealogy is not a normal biological line: it is a spliced honorary genealogy that grafts Samuel's Ephraimite posterity into a Levitical genealogy as an honor for Samuel's service. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=890" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:50</a> | Is that how honorary degrees work too? | He said honorary degrees are similar in that they honor someone as if they were a doctor, but they are not the same as earning a doctorate. | He compares an honorary doctorate to being treated as "doctor for a day": the recipient is honored as though they were a doctor because of accomplishment, support, or status, but they have not actually completed a doctorate. He stresses that everyone should understand it as honor and respect rather than a real earned degree, even though some people in the church mistakenly treat honorary doctorates as equivalent to earned ones. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=1005" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:45</a> | What are your thoughts on the announcement that Linear Elamite has been deciphered? | He was excited by the possibility because decipherable Elamite texts could matter greatly for ancient Near Eastern chronology, but he still wanted to evaluate the paper carefully. | He says a real decipherment would be enormously important because many Linear Elamite texts exist, but they had lacked a Rosetta-Stone-like key. He notes that a multi-institution team has worked on the problem, which makes the claim more promising, but he still wants to test the paper and see whether the proposed readings actually pan out. If they do, he thinks the material could illuminate dark areas of Elamite history and ancient Near Eastern chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=1195" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:55</a> | In Judges 11, could Jephthah have used Leviticus 27:2-8 or Leviticus 5:4-6 as a way out of his rash oath? | He said yes; those laws likely offered possible exits, and Jephthah's failure showed his ignorance of Torah. | He says Leviticus 27:2-8 appears to allow a life bound by a vow to be redeemed for a set valuation, while Leviticus 5:4-6 provides an exit strategy and sin offering for a rash oath. He therefore thinks these were plausible ways out, but Jephthah and his village seem ignorant of Torah and shaped by regional customs instead. For him the episode illustrates the failure of Torah instruction being passed down and Jephthah's status as a bad, unholy, and unlearned judge. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=1381" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:01</a> | What is your view of the deuterocanonical additions to Daniel, such as the Hymn of the Three Youths? | He did not regard them as part of the original Daniel because they are absent from the Masoretic tradition and the Dead Sea Scrolls, though he allowed they may be older apocryphal material. | He says he finally examined the issue and does not think the additions were part of the original Daniel. His reason is textual: they do not appear in the Masoretic text or in the Dead Sea Scrolls Daniel material, and instead appear only in parts of the Septuagint tradition. He allows that the material may be old and perhaps came through an Egyptian apocryphal stream, but he does not regard it as Scripture or as part of Daniel's original literary tradition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=1583" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:23</a> | Do you think John the Immerser would have been an Essene? | He said it is highly debated and difficult to know; John came from a Levitical family, but the evidence does not securely place him in an Essene faction. | He says John's connection to the Essenes is highly debated. John was the son of a Levite, but that alone does not make him an Essene, and he thinks the evidence for the Essenes themselves is murky. He also questions the common assumption that Qumran was simply an Essene sanctuary or monastery, leaning instead toward seeing it as a manuscript-production site with discarded or unusable texts, so he does not think the evidence securely places John in that sect. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=2043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:03</a> | Was the curse from Jeremiah 22:24-30 reversed in Haggai 2:23, or was it limited to Jehoiachin's lifetime? | He said the curse was not simply reversed; Zerubbabel could function as a leader, but the Davidic royal line remained cut off from ruling as kings until the messianic branch. | He reads Jeremiah's curse in light of Isaiah 11: the Davidic tree is cut down so that the royal line is cut off from kingship and authority, even if descendants remain. Zerubbabel can return as a leader or governor under another power and can be likened to a signet ring in Haggai, but he does not restore the old Davidic kingship. The hope comes through the small branch from the stump, which he identifies with the Messiah whose kingdom has no end. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=2553" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:33</a> | How did ancient Egyptian color terms work? | He said Egyptian had a limited standard color vocabulary, with little linguistic nuance for things like warm and cool colors, while artistic practice was shaped heavily by materials and pragmatics. | He describes a basic Egyptian color vocabulary with terms for white, yellow or gold, red, green, blue or turquoise, and black, while noting that scribal palettes could contain more pigments than the written terms label. He says they did not linguistically distinguish warm and cool colors, had no separate terms for orange or purple, and treated gold and yellow together. Artistically, Egyptians could mix colors and usually painted things in practical or natural-looking colors, but he cautions against overreading color symbolism because materials, surfaces, deterioration, and available media strongly shaped the art. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=2929" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:49</a> | What do you make of Graham Hancock's claim that the Sphinx was built long before the ancient Egyptians? | He rejected the claim strongly, saying the archaeological context ties the Sphinx to Khafre's complex and gives no support to Hancock's proposal. | He rejects Hancock's proposal as unsupported by the site context. In his view, it makes no sense to imagine an isolated ancient Sphinx already sitting on the Giza plateau while Khufu and later builders ignored that context. He points instead to the Sphinx's integration with Khafre's mortuary temple, causeway, pyramid complex, and nearby port, and says the archaeological context gives no evidence for Hancock's much earlier date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=3092" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:32</a> | If the root of YHWH-yireh refers to seeing rather than providing, how should it be translated? | He said "the Lord will provide" is possible but not exhaustive; the root can carry ideas of seeing, appearing, manifesting, or making something appear. | He says the Hebrew root has a broad semantic range including seeing, being seen, appearing, making oneself known, and manifesting. Because Hebrew does not work by one fixed English equivalent per word, he resists giving a single concordant translation. "The Lord will provide" is possible, but he also suggests possibilities such as the Lord who makes things appear, the Lord who appears, or the Lord of manifestations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=3283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:43</a> | What does 2 Samuel 7:6 mean? | He said the passage contrasts David wanting to build God a house with God promising to build a house for Israel and David's line. | He reads the verse in the context of 2 Samuel 7:5-11. God says he has never asked Israel to build him a cedar house, and the point is that from the Exodus through David's time God has been making a place and a house for Israel. Only later does the passage speak of David's descendant building a house and God establishing his kingdom, so the immediate contrast is between David's desire to build for God and God's prior commitment to build for David and Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=3591" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:51</a> | How can we be certain Late Bronze Hazor was destroyed in the 13th century BC rather than the 12th? | He said the pottery in the destruction layer, especially Mycenaean IIIC ware, anchors it firmly in the late 13th century. | He says the dating rests on ceramic typology in a clean archaeological stratum. The destruction layer at Hazor contains Mycenaean IIIC material, which he says dates without real wiggle room to the late 13th century and Late Bronze IIB. Because the stratigraphy is unusually clear, he treats the pottery in and around Stratum 1A as firm evidence against moving the destruction into the 12th century. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=3726" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:06</a> | Does Deuteronomy 11:4 suggest Egypt suffered a severe decline because of the Exodus, or is that hyperbole? | He said it refers especially to the destruction of Egypt's army, which compromised Egypt's hegemonic control, though he cautioned against overreading it as total national collapse. | He says Deuteronomy 11:4 is focused on what happened to Egypt's army, horses, and chariots rather than claiming the whole nation instantly collapsed. Still, he thinks the loss of those forces would have marked the beginning of Egypt's weakened hegemonic control in the Levant. He points to later pressure from Philistines, Israelites, Libyans, and the loss of control by the time of later Ramesside kings, while cautioning that the decline should not be overstated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=4058" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:38</a> | Does Dwayne Garrett's reading of Exodus 1:16 as a euphemism for male sex organs work? | He rejected the reading and said the "stones" are birthing bricks, an Egyptian childbirth practice reflected in the passage. | He rejects Garrett's reading as wrong and says the dual "stones" in Exodus 1:16 are not a euphemism for infant male sex organs. He argues the verse refers to Egyptian birthing bricks: two stones or bricks on which a woman stood during childbirth so gravity could help while the midwife caught the child. He treats this as an Egyptian cultural artifact reflected in the Pentateuch and points to known examples of decorated birth bricks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=4460" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:20</a> | Is Vespasian the typological man of lawlessness? | He said Vespasian can be a type, but not the archetype or final consummation; figures such as Antiochus Epiphanes and Nero also function as types. | He says it is difficult to speak with certainty, but there can be many types of the man of lawlessness. He identifies Nero as a major typological figure and compares him with Antiochus Epiphanes; Vespasian can also be one type along the way. But he distinguishes a type from the final consummation and says Vespasian is neither the archetype nor the final man of lawlessness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=4593" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:33</a> | Do Christians who hold theistic evolution hurt the gospel? | He said he is not a fan of either theistic evolution or young-earth creationism as harmonizing systems, but he did not think theistic evolution automatically destroys Christianity. | He says he dislikes compatibilizing systems that try to force secular science and biblical interpretation into the same model, and he applies that criticism to both theistic evolution and young-earth creationism. He does not think theistic evolution necessarily hurts Christianity, because its effect depends on the particular form: the worst versions can approach deism or naturalism, while the best can be Christological, contextual, and more biblical than some young-earth positions. He also warns that some young-earth creationism harms the gospel by tying faith to one fragile interpretation of Genesis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=5065" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:25</a> | Were the Canaanites being punished in the conquest, or mainly driven out as Walton argues? | He mostly agreed with Walton that the key point is dispossession and expulsion rather than a simple punishment model. | He says Walton's view has real merit, especially the idea that the conquest language is about driving out or setting apart the Canaanites so Israel can inherit the land. He does not treat Walton's reading as perfect or exclusive, but he thinks it is one of the strongest available views. He emphasizes that the Canaanites knew Israel was coming, feared them, and were effectively being told to leave rather than simply being punished as sinners. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=5236" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:16</a> | Why did God appoint unrighteous judges? Were there slim pickings during those times? | He said yes in some respects; Israel was often in a degraded state, and God sometimes used deeply flawed people who were nevertheless able to act in that moment. | He says the situation in Judges often really was slim pickings. Using Jephthah and Samson as examples, he describes Israel as so degraded that leaders turned to deeply flawed men because they were the ones able to act against oppression. He broadens the point by saying God has often used broken or unsavory people in biblical and church history to accomplish needed work, while still insisting their flaws should not be treated as role models. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=5789" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:29</a> | What would it have meant to be excluded from the assembly of the Lord in Deuteronomy 23:1-8? | He said it meant exclusion from direct participation in the temple or tabernacle precincts, though some worship and obligations could still be handled through proxies. | He explains the assembly as those with access to the temple or tabernacle precincts for direct ritual participation. Exclusion did not necessarily bar all home rituals such as Passover, but it did bar someone from entering the relevant holy courts to bring offerings personally. Such people could still use a proxy, including a kinsman, to bring sacrifices, tithes, or taxes, so the exclusion was a real social and cultic restriction but not an absolute end to all participation or obligations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=6333" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:33</a> | How do scholars find the right books and journals to stay current? | He said scholars rely on society memberships, peer-reviewed journals, libraries, book reviews, and heavy repeated reading that makes later work faster to assess. | He says scholars use practical systems: joining academic societies for their peer-reviewed journals, using university and community library access, and taking book reviews that force them to read and produce concise evaluations. Journals matter because they contain the newest research, while books and repeated reading build background knowledge. The more one reads in a field, the faster later books become because much of the material is already familiar and only the new research requires close attention. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=7131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:51</a> | What are your thoughts on increased anti-Trinitarian sentiment among Western Christians? | He said anti-Trinitarianism is outside the historic Christian faith because the Trinity is foundational to the Christian understanding of God. | He says anti-Trinitarianism is outside the historic Christian faith because Christianity has long confessed Father, Son, and Holy Spirit as three persons who are the one God. He names groups and tendencies that reject or distort the Trinity and says that once someone adopts a non-Trinitarian view of God and Christ, they have crossed a dividing line into something other than Christianity. For him, a Trinitarian worldview follows inevitably from Scripture's description of God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=7405" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:25</a> | What was the darkness of the ninth plague, and is it related to the darkness at the crucifixion? | He treated the plague darkness as materially different, perhaps involving a dense sandstorm or ash-laden event, rather than the same phenomenon as the crucifixion darkness. | He distinguishes the ninth plague from the darkness at the crucifixion, which he thinks may have involved the sun being darkened. In Exodus, the darkness seems materially thick, entering homes and preventing even indoor light from giving safety. He speculates cautiously that it may have involved a sandstorm combined with ash or fine particulates in the atmosphere, noting that Egyptian texts also describe episodes where the sun is blotted out and darkness covers the land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=7782" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:42</a> | What is the firmament in Genesis 1:7, and what water did they think was above it? | He described the firmament as the rakia, using analogical language for a celestial ocean or water above the sky, while leaving physical mechanisms such as clouds or canopy theories open. | He describes the rakia as analogical language for an expanse or dome associated with a celestial ocean. The logic is that people saw water below, saw a blue sky above, and saw water falling from the sky, so Genesis speaks of God separating waters above from waters below. He leaves questions such as clouds versus canopy theories to others, but says the basic idea is water in the sky, framed as the celestial ocean above the firmament. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=8100" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:00</a> | Can you expand on Genesis 1 being about a temple? | He said Genesis 1 and 2 use cultic and temple imagery: ordered spaces, filled spaces, humanity as God's image, and Eden as sacred space guarded after the fall. | He says Genesis 1 and 2 are full of cultic or temple language. The seven days form three spaces that are ordered and then filled, paralleling temple spatial ideas; humanity is created as God's image and likeness, using words associated with cultic images or idols in a temple, but God fills those images with his own spirit. Eden then functions as a holy-of-holies-like sacred space, and after the fall cherubim and the flaming sword guard that space from defilement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=8629" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:49</a> | How historical is the Old Testament, did everyone exist, and how does the resurrection of the dead work? | He said the Old Testament is historical in its ancient literary context, with some figures personified or aggregated, and he held that both believers and unbelievers are resurrected bodily. | He says the Old Testament is historical in its own ancient context, but readers must account for genre and literary devices. Some figures are personifications, such as Wisdom in Proverbs, while others may be aggregates, such as Pharaoh as a literary unity representing several kings, or clan figures whose long lifespans express clan identity rather than one continuous individual life. On resurrection, he says everyone is resurrected bodily: believers receive bodies conformed to Christ and blessing, while unbelievers receive bodies fit for judgment, because humans are meant to be embodied rather than disembodied spirits. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=9177" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:57</a> | Did you see the trailer for the new Indiana Jones movie, and are you excited for it? | He had not seen the trailer yet and said he was not excited, mainly because he thought the previous film was so poor. | He says he had not yet seen the trailer and had only heard about it that day. His expectations were low because he considered the previous Indiana Jones film extremely bad and thought the franchise had been getting worse over time. He says he was not excited and felt more fear and trembling about the new film than anticipation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=9290" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:50</a> | In what city did Pharaoh's daughter find Moses? | He answered Avaris, arguing that "Pharaoh's daughter" is a retrojective title and that the adoption fits the daughter of the vizier before her father became king. | He answers Avaris and explains the historical reasoning behind it. In his reconstruction, "Pharaoh's daughter" is a retrojective title: at the time of the adoption she was the daughter of the vizier, not yet the daughter of the king, because Egyptian royal daughters normally could not adopt on their own while other free women could. He identifies the relevant vizier as General Ramses, later Ramses I, whose daughter could have adopted Moses at Avaris in Goshen, a delta city with the reeds and papyrus setting implied by the story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=9599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:59</a> | Were Horemheb and Ramses I the same person? | He said no; they were colleagues and close military associates, but distinct people. | He says Horemheb and Ramses I were not the same person. They were colleagues, friends, and military associates who served on campaigns together, with Horemheb as the higher-ranking general. Horemheb made Ramses I crown prince and successor while still alive, but their close relationship does not make them identical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=9674" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:14</a> | Based on Genesis 2:8-14, where did the author think Eden and the Garden of Eden were located? | He said the geographic markers point most likely to Mesopotamia, though one river remains unidentified and the region may now be inaccessible for excavation. | He says the Genesis river markers most likely point to Mesopotamia, especially because the Tigris and Euphrates are identifiable and he reads the Gihon reference as Kish rather than Cush due to a later vowel issue. He says one river remains unidentified, but three markers point in the same general region. He also notes that the southern Mesopotamian delta has changed as water levels moved in, so the area where the garden might have been may not be practically excavable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=10088" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:08</a> | What was the purpose of God saying man's years would be 120 in Genesis 6:3? | He favored the view that it was a 120-year countdown until the flood, not a statement of maximum human lifespan. | He contrasts two interpretations: a maximum human lifespan of 120 years, or a countdown before judgment. He favors the second view, saying the statement means Noah has 120 years to finish the ark before judgment falls on humanity. In that reading, the verse is not primarily a biological limit but a warning period before the flood. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AgLundgwtWs?t=10174" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:34</a> | Wasn't it a cherubim that was placed at Eden? | He said both cherubim and the flaming sword are present, interpreting the flaming sword as a description of seraphim guarding the Tree of Life. | He says Genesis 3:24 includes both cherubim and the flaming sword, not a cherub holding a sword. In his reading the flaming sword is a descriptive way to refer to seraphim, fiery beings guarding the way to the Tree of Life from all directions. He thinks the author uses description rather than naming them "seraphim" to avoid confusing them with the serpent, so the scene includes cherubim, seraphim, and the nakash as distinct beings. |
